summarization.a: The figure shows multiple infrared difference spectra of a B2O3 ALD film recorded after consecutive HF exposures at 150 °C. A sharp H–F stretching feature appears after HF dosing, while the B–O–H band decreases, indicating loss of specific hydroxyl configurations. Simultaneously, a broad hydrogen-bonded O–H band deepens with increasing HF exposure, reflecting progressive changes in surface O–H bonding.

data_extraction.a:
| Wavenumber region (cm⁻¹) | Vibrational assignment | Evolution with HF exposure |
|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| ~3750–3800 | H–F stretch | Sharp positive peak appears after initial HF exposure |
| ~3700 | B–O–H stretch | Negative feature indicating loss of B–O–H species |
| ~3650–3450 | H-bonded O–H | Broad negative band that deepens and broadens with repeated HF exposures (cycles 4–9) |

a.question: What do the changes in the O–H stretching region reveal about the surface during repeated HF exposure?
a.answer: The increasing depth and width of the O–H stretching band indicate that the surface O–H environment becomes more strongly hydrogen-bonded as HF exposure continues. At the same time, the negative B–O–H feature shows depletion of specific hydroxyl configurations present in the initial film. Together, these trends reflect progressive modification of surface O–H bonding during repeated HF exposure.

a.question: Which spectral feature corresponds to the H–F stretching vibration?
How does the B–O–H related feature near 3700 cm⁻¹ change with HF exposure?
a.answer: It decreases in intensity, indicating loss of B–O–H species.

a.question: Do the infrared spectra indicate increasing surface modification after multiple HF cycles?
